steps.title: Identify condition for no solution
steps.content:
The left‑hand side is $$y = -13x - \frac{1}{9}$$.
For a generic choice the right‑hand side is $$y = mx + c$$.
Setting them equal gives $$(-13-m)x + \left(-\frac{1}{9}-c\right)=0$$.
No solution occurs when the coefficient of $$x$$ is zero and the constant term is non‑zero: $$-13-m=0$$ and $$-\frac{1}{9}-c \neq 0$$.
That simplifies to $$m = -13$$ and $$c \neq -\frac{1}{9}$$
Thus we need a choice with the same slope $$-13$$ but a different constant term.

steps.title: Test the choices for equal slope and different intercept
steps.content:
We examine each choice's right‑hand side for slope $$m$$ and intercept $$c$$.
  • Choice A: $$y = -13x - \frac{1}{9}$$ → $$m = -13$$, $$c = -\frac{1}{9}$$ → same slope and same intercept → infinitely many solutions.
  • Choice B: $$y = -13x + \frac{1}{9}$$ → $$m = -13$$, $$c = +\frac{1}{9}$$ → same slope, different intercept → parallel distinct lines → no solution.
  • Choice C: $$y = 13x - \frac{1}{9}$$ → $$m = +13$$ → slope differs → lines intersect once.
  • Choice D: $$y = 13x + \frac{1}{9}$$ → $$m = +13$$ → slope differs → lines intersect once.
Only choice B meets the no‑solution condition.
That matches choice B — B is correct.
